title: Training vs Inference — Inference Scaling and Test-Time Compute
key_insight: The newest frontier: scaling inference compute (thinking longer) may be as impactful as scaling training compute, fundamentally changing the economics of AI deployment.
body:
## The Inference Scaling Hypothesis

For decades, AI progress = more training compute. The 2024 insight:

```
Test-time compute (thinking at inference) can substitute for training compute.

OpenAI o1 (2024):
  - Uses chain-of-thought 'reasoning traces' of variable length
  - Harder problems → longer reasoning → better answers
  - Performance on math/code problems far exceeds GPT-4 on standard inference
  - At the cost of: slower response, higher per-token cost
```

## Speculative Decoding: Free Inference Speedup

```python
# Problem: large model generates tokens slowly (one at a time)
# Solution: use a small fast model to draft, large model to verify

# Speculative decoding algorithm:
# 1. Small model (draft): generates K tokens quickly (e.g. K=5)
# 2. Large model (verifier): runs ONE forward pass on all K tokens
# 3. Large model accepts tokens it agrees with, rejects the first disagreement
# 4. Repeat from the rejection point

# Why it's a speedup:
# - Large model runs 1 forward pass instead of K sequential passes
# - If draft model agrees with large model most of the time: ~3x speedup
# - Output distribution is provably identical to sampling from large model alone

from transformers import pipeline
# HuggingFace implementation:
result = large_pipe(
    'The meaning of life is',
    assistant_model=small_model,  # speculative decoding
)
```

## Quantisation: The Training-Inference Trade-off

```python
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig

# Training: fp32 (4 bytes/param) or fp16 (2 bytes/param)
# Inference: can use int8 (1 byte) or int4 (0.5 bytes) with small quality loss

quant_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,  # compute in bf16, store in 4-bit
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type='nf4',  # Normal Float 4 — optimal for normally distributed weights
)

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    'meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B',
    quantization_config=quant_config,
)
# Result: 8B param model in 4-bit = ~4.5GB instead of ~16GB
# Quality degradation: typically <2% on most benchmarks
```

## The True Cost Model

```
Total cost of an AI system:
  Training cost (one-time):        $X
  Inference cost (ongoing/day):    $Y × days_deployed

  For a 3-year product life:
  Total = X + 365×3×Y

  For most products after launch:
  inference >> training in total cost

  Therefore:
  - Optimising training = reducing X (one-time)
  - Optimising inference = reducing Y (ongoing, multiplied by usage)

  At high usage, inference optimisation has higher ROI than training efficiency
```
common_mistakes: Measuring only training cost when evaluating model options — inference cost over 3 years is almost always larger
Quantising without benchmarking — quality degradation is task-dependent; always test on your specific task
Using speculative decoding with a poor draft model — if draft accuracy < 70%, overhead exceeds benefit
mini_exercise: Calculate: if a company runs inference on a 70B model serving 100,000 requests/day, at $0.001/token and average 500 output tokens/request — what is the monthly inference cost? Now calculate how it changes if they switch to 4-bit quantisation that cuts memory in half (allowing 2x the batch size, halving the per-token cost). What's the annual saving?
